narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, but let me walk you through the story the admissions model saw in your application, like piecing together chapters of a book that didn't quite align for bar exam success. Your first-year decile of 1.0 stood out as the biggest signal, painting a picture of early struggles in a competitive law school environment that often foreshadow tough roads ahead on the bar. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 31 whispered the same cautionary tale, suggesting the analytical firepower needed for those grueling exams might be a stretch. Even your third-year decile of 1.0 reinforced this pattern, showing the challenge persisted despite time to adjust. Surprisingly, your high family income didn't tip the scales toward stability as it does for some—it actually nudged the outlook downward in this mix, perhaps because it highlighted other gaps the model weighs heavily. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 added to the weight, indicating a foundation that hasn't yet proven sturdy enough for the bar's demands. As a white male going full-time, those details each pulled a bit more toward the risk of failure, weaving a complete narrative of an 80% predicted chance of not passing. It's not about your potential or heart—it's this specific combination that echoed too many past stories of students who hit walls at the bar. The model isn't judging you; it's just reading the patterns from thousands like you. If you strengthen those early grades or LSAT through retakes or targeted prep, the story could rewrite itself dramatically next time. You've got the drive—channel it, and doors will open. Hang in there.
